mūla.devanāgarī: श्रद्धया परया तप्तं तपस् तत् त्रिविधं नरैः | अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिर् युक्तैः सात्त्विकं परिचक्षते
mūla.iast: śraddhayā parayā taptaṃ tapas tat trividhaṃ naraiḥ | aphalākāṅkṣibhir yuktaiḥ sāttvikaṃ paricakṣate
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 17 (Śraddhātraya-Vibhāga-Yoga (The Yoga of Distinction of Threefold Faith)), verse 17
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: आस्तिक्यबुद्ध्या परया प्रकृष्टया तप्तम् अनुष्ठितं तपः

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If shraddha (faith) must be 'supreme' for tapas to be sattvika, what contaminates ordinary faith — doubt about scripture, doubt about the method, or desire for the fruit?
The verse attributes sattvika tapas to nara-s (persons) collectively — does this imply that community or sangha is a pre-condition for the quality of austerity, not just an incidental setting?

Aphala-akanksha (desirelessness of fruit) appears as a prerequisite, not a result — how does a practitioner cultivate desirelessness before the tapas rather than hoping it will emerge after sustained practice?

Each school reads yukta differently: Shankara reads it as samahita (composed), Ramanuja as chinta-yukta (God-remembering), Sridhara as ekagra-chitta (single-pointed) — what does the choice of gloss reveal about each school's theory of what the mind must be doing during tapas?
The verse covers body, speech, and mind as one package — but daily practice rarely advances uniformly across all three. Which lag typically exposes the practitioner to rajasic or tamasic drift?
If this sattvika standard is difficult to meet, does falling short invalidate the tapas entirely, or does partial shraddha with partial desirelessness still produce partial purification?

everyday_applications.advaita: Before beginning any sustained practice — exercise, fasting, silence — examine whether the motivation is 'this will make me better' (fruit-seeking) or simply the conviction that this is what sattva requires right now. The Shankara test: would you continue the practice identically if you were guaranteed it would produce no visible result? If not, the shraddha is mixed with rajas.
everyday_applications.visistadvaita: Reframe the mental narration during physical tapas — exercise, dietary discipline, service — from 'I am improving myself' to 'I am offering this body's effort to Bhagavan.' Ramanuja's chinta-yukta standard means the God-remembering is simultaneous with the action, not a before-and-after bookend. Try holding one line of a prayer or name during a physical discipline and notice whether the quality of effort shifts.
everyday_applications.dvaita: When a long practice or project produces no visible fruit, test whether the equanimity is genuine or suppressed disappointment. The Madhva reading implies the jiva knows in advance it is not the real agent — so neither success nor failure touches the core identity. Journaling the attribution ('I did this' vs 'this moved through me toward Hari') after outcomes reveals how deep the paratantrata recognition actually goes.
everyday_applications.suddhadvaita: Notice whether your strongest devotional or creative states feel self-generated or received. Vallabha's pushti-marga asks practitioners to distinguish effortful shraddha from the spontaneous shraddha that arises when grace is present — the latter feels like remembering rather than constructing. Treat periods of dry, effortful practice as diagnostic: they may signal a mismatch between what you are offering and what Krishna is actually calling you toward.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's ekagra-chitta (single-pointed mind) test for yukta: during your primary practice, count how many times the mind produces a fruit-image (what this will lead to, how others will perceive it, what you will gain). Even a single fruit-image is evidence that the chitta is not yet ekagra. The discipline is not suppression of those images but returning without drama each time — the returning IS the tapas.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's 'unmoved by success or failure' criterion applies directly to creative and professional work. After completing a significant effort, observe the internal weather at the moment of receiving feedback — elation at praise, deflation at criticism. Both are phala-abhisandhi (fruit-clinging) made visible. The sattvika practitioner's internal weather at outcome-receipt looks identical to the weather during the work itself: steady, quiet, unspectacular.
